title: Spinning a worsted weight on the wheel
subtitle: A beginner guide to spinning consistent aran-to-worsted weight singles from combed top
excerpt: Spin a consistent worsted-weight singles from commercially prepared combed top on a spinning wheel. This entry covers setting the wheel for worsted weight, controlling grist, and checking the wraps per inch to produce a balanced aran-to-worsted singles ready for plying.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional spinning practice. Spinning a consistent worsted weight on the wheel is a foundational skill documented across British handspinning guild curricula.

glossaryTerms.definition: A spinning method in which fibres are drawn from a combed preparation with the hand held close to the orifice, producing a smooth, dense, low-loft yarn.
A measurement of yarn grist: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler or card in one inch of width. Worsted weight yarn typically wraps 12 to 18 times per inch.
The weight-to-length ratio of a yarn, describing its thickness. In handspinning, grist is checked by wraps per inch or by weighing a measured length against a standard reference.

content.text: Worsted weight is the most versatile yarn weight a handspinner produces: thick enough to knit up quickly, fine enough to show stitch definition. Using the
from combed top, the yarn will be smooth, dense, and consistent. The key skill is checking
throughout the bobbins, so the plied yarn is even from start to finish.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing away from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Combed top, any breed (Corriedale or BFL work well for beginners)

content.text: For worsted weight singles, set a flyer ratio of 6:1 to 8:1 and moderate uptake tension. Too fast a ratio creates an over-twisted, wiry singles; too slow allows the yarn to drift apart. Set up the wheel and spin a test length of about 30 cm before beginning the main bobbin.

Pre-draft the top to about twice its original diameter before beginning. Hold the pre-drafted top in the back hand and draft outward using the worsted draw, allowing only the front hand to let twist in. The draft zone should be no longer than the staple length of the fibre. Keep the treadle at an even, moderate pace.

Every 15 to 20 minutes, wind a short length around your wpi gauge or ruler. Worsted weight singles at this stage should wrap 16 to 20 times per inch; after plying, the two-ply will wrap 12 to 18 times per inch. Adjust by drafting out a little further (for finer) or letting slightly more fibre through per pass (for thicker).

Fill two bobbins, then ply from the lazy kate in the reverse direction. Plied worsted weight should feel balanced in a Andean ply test: fold the plied yarn in half and let it rest; a balanced two-ply will sit straight, with no kinking in either direction. Set the twist in warm water and hang to dry.

items.problem: The singles are inconsistent in thickness across the bobbin
items.cause: Pre-drafting was uneven, or the draft zone varied in length
items.fix: Pre-draft more thoroughly and maintain a consistent distance from the back hand to the orifice on each stroke.

items.problem: The two-ply kinks when a length is held folded
items.cause: The singles were over-twisted relative to the plying twist
items.fix: Ply with slightly more twist to balance the singles, or reduce the singles twist on the next bobbin.
